description_long: This is a large, upright stone guard marker with a roughly oval, irregular shape and a weathered, natural surface. The stone is predominantly gray with patches of lighter discoloration and some moss or lichen growth near its base, blending into a ground covering of gravel and sparse vegetation. One face of the stone features a series of inscribed characters, likely historical or commemorative, etched vertically into the surface. The other sides are unadorned, showing only the natural texture and coloration of the stone. The object appears solid and heavy, with a slightly tapered top and a broad, stable base. Its overall appearance suggests it serves as a protective or boundary marker in an urban or historical setting.
description: A large, upright gray stone with weathered surfaces and inscribed characters on one face, set on gravel with some vegetation at the base.
description_view_0: The stone's broad, rounded side is visible, showing natural weathering and some lichen, with no visible inscriptions.
description_view_1: This view reveals the inscribed face, with vertical lines of characters etched into the stone's surface.
description_view_2: The narrower end of the stone is shown, highlighting its irregular, tapered top and rough texture.
description_view_3: The back of the stone is visible, showing a dark, unmarked surface with some moss at the base.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the edge of urban sidewalks, near the entrance to driveways, historical sites, or at the corners of streets to protect structures or mark boundaries.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be installed fully above ground with its base set firmly on or slightly into the ground for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible surface smoothing and discoloration from prolonged weather exposure, with some chipping and minor erosion likely caused by vehicle contact or foot traffic at the base. Lichen and moss growth are present near the bottom, indicating moisture retention and age.
attribute_historical_markings: Vertical inscriptions with multiple characters are etched into one face of the stone, likely in an East Asian script, suggesting commemorative or historical significance.
